result:
Prompt caching lets an LLM API store the processed internal state (the computed key/value attention tensors) for a chunk of input tokens—typically a long, stable prefix like a system prompt, tool definitions, or a document. On later requests that reuse that exact prefix, the API skips recomputing it and reads from cache instead.

Cached input tokens are billed at a discount (often ~90% off) because the expensive work—running those tokens through the model's forward pass—was already done and stored. The provider only pays to fetch state, not recompute it, so it passes savings on. It also lowers latency.

One practical thing to do: **put your stable content first and your variable content last.** Caching matches from the start of the prompt, so a fixed system prompt/context block placed before the changing user turn maximizes cache hits. Reordering so variability lives at the tail is the single highest-leverage change.
